2026-02-06

library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(GGally)
library(MASS)
library(klaR)
library(dismo)

Below is a template to get you started on the Unit 3 Project. Make sure you edit the metadata above to include your name. For questions that include code, the template to produce R chunks is below. Any code you write that produces an output must be fully visible in the HTML document you hand in!

Notes (2/10/26)

Reproductive Isolation:

  • prezygotic (before the fusion of the gametes) –> mechanical, temporal, habitat/spatial, behavioral
  • allopatric speciation –> change in habitat where there is a geographical barrier that causes a speciation event
  • sympatric speciation –> speciation that can occur without a geographical barrier
  • autopolyploid and allopolyploid

Part I: Morphological Data

Notes

linear discriminate analysis –> we use this when we want to combine multiple data points to say something about the data

Question 1

mean_table <- iris %>% 
  group_by(Species) %>% 
  summarise(
    avg_sepal_len = mean(Sepal.Length),
    avg_sepal_wid = mean(Sepal.Width),
    avg_petal_len = mean(Petal.Length),
    avg_petal_wid = mean(Petal.Width)
    
  )
print(mean_table)
## # A tibble: 3 × 5
##   Species    avg_sepal_len avg_sepal_wid avg_petal_len avg_petal_wid
##   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>         <dbl>         <dbl>
## 1 setosa              5.01          3.43          1.46         0.246
## 2 versicolor          5.94          2.77          4.26         1.33 
## 3 virginica           6.59          2.97          5.55         2.03

It seems like there are some values that are significantly different, especially regarding the setosa iris, where it seems to be larger in every variable but the sepal length on average.

Question 2

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Width, y = Petal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Anderson's Iris Data", x = "Petal Width", y = "Petal Length") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Width, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Anderson's Iris Data", x = "Petal Width", y = "Sepal Width") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Width, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Anderson's Iris Data", x = "Petal Width", y = "Sepal Length") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Petal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Anderson's Iris Data", x = "Sepal Width", y = "Petal Length") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Petal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Anderson's Iris Data", x = "Sepal Length", y = "Petal Length") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Length, y = Petal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Anderson's Iris Data", x = "Petal Length", y = "Petal Width") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Petal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Anderson's Iris Data", x = "Sepal Width", y = "Petal Width") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Petal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Anderson's Iris Data", x = "Sepal Length", y = "Petal.Width") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Length, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Anderson's Iris Data", x = "Petal Length", y = "Sepal Width") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Length, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Anderson's Iris Data", x = "Petal Length", y = "Sepal Length") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) +
geom_point() + labs(title = "Anderson's Iris Data", x = "Sepal Width", y = "Sepal Length") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Anderson's Iris Data", x = "Sepal Length", y = "Sepal Width")+ theme_minimal()

Question 3

SepalLandPetalL <- iris %>%
    dplyr::select(Sepal.Length:Petal.Length)

Species_iris <- iris %>%
    pull(Species)

fitSLPL <- lda(SepalLandPetalL, Species_iris)
pred.fitSLPL <- predict(fitSLPL)
predictionSLPL <- pred.fitSLPL$class

prediction.tableSLPL <- table(iris$Species, predictionSLPL)
prediction.tableSLPL
##             predictionSLPL
##              setosa versicolor virginica
##   setosa         50          0         0
##   versicolor      0         48         2
##   virginica       0          3        47
new.irisSLPL <- iris %>%
    dplyr::select(Species) %>%
    add_column(predictionSLPL)
plot(fitSLPL, col = c("yellow2", "green3", "purple1")[unclass(new.irisSLPL$Species)], xlim = c(-5, 0))

#-----
PetalLandW <- iris %>%
  dplyr::select(Petal.Length:Petal.Width)

fitPLW <- lda(PetalLandW, Species_iris)
pred.fitPLW <- predict(fitPLW)
predictionPLW <- pred.fitPLW$class

prediction.tablePLW <- table(iris$Species, predictionPLW)
prediction.tablePLW
##             predictionPLW
##              setosa versicolor virginica
##   setosa         50          0         0
##   versicolor      0         48         2
##   virginica       0          4        46
new.irisPLW <- iris %>%
    dplyr::select(Species) %>%
    add_column(predictionPLW)
plot(fitPLW, col = c("yellow2", "green3", "purple1")[unclass(new.irisPLW$Species)], xlim = c(-5, 0))

In the previous question it looked like the two graphs with sepal length and petal length, as well as petal length and petal width seemed to be the best separated for species out of all the other graphs. When making a prediction table, it seemed that these measurements are not perfect predictors, but can estimate the species fairly well. It seems that the comparison between sepal length and petal length is slightly more accurate.

Question 4

I used the LDA analysis to determine which measurement out of the two I chose predicts most accurately the species based on the data. Based on this data, I believe Edgar Anderson’s predictions were fairly accurate, but it is possible that he was mistaken in some classification of certain individuals, similar to the prediction model due to their similarily. Though, there are many other factors in speciation that do not necessarily depend on sepals and petals, and perhaps there are other identifiers for different species that he considered.

Part II: Geographical and temporal data

library(dismo)

get.gbif <- function(genus, species, na.rm = TRUE, crop = TRUE) {
    # GBIF query
    gps <- gbif(genus, species, geo = FALSE)
    # drop NA entries; note that this option is visited only with na.rm=TRUE
    if (na.rm) {
        gps <- subset(gps, !is.na(lon) & !is.na(lat))
    }
    if (crop) {
        keep <- c(which(colnames(gps) == "species"), which(colnames(gps) == "lat"), which(colnames(gps) == "lon"), which(colnames(gps) ==
            "month"))
        gps <- gps[keep]
    }
    return(gps)
}
library(maps)

Question 1

vanilla_planifolia <- get.gbif("Vanilla", "planifolia")
vanilla_pompona <- get.gbif("Vanilla", "pompona")
vanilla_tahitensis <- get.gbif("Vanilla","tahitensis")

vanilla_orchid_gps <- bind_rows(vanilla_planifolia,vanilla_pompona,vanilla_tahitensis)
write_csv(vanilla_orchid_gps, file = "vanilla_orchid_gps.csv", col_names = TRUE)
vanilla_orchid_gps <- read_csv("vanilla_orchid_gps.csv")

library(maps)

ggplot2::theme_set(theme_bw())
world.map <- map_data(map="world")
mexico.map <- subset(world.map, region == "Mexico")

ggplot(world.map, aes(long,lat, group=group)) + geom_polygon(fill = "white", color = "gray90") + coord_quickmap() +geom_point(data= vanilla_orchid_gps, mapping = aes(x = lon, y = lat, group = species, color = species), size = 0.5) 

ggplot(mexico.map, aes(long,lat, group=group)) + geom_polygon(fill = "white", color = "gray90") + coord_quickmap(xlim = c(-118,-86), ylim = c(14,33)) + geom_point(data= vanilla_orchid_gps, mapping = aes(x = lon, y = lat, group = species, color = species), size = 0.5)

I chose to display three vanilla orchid species. It seems like two species occur around the same areas, with Vanilla planifolia being more common worldwide. Vanilla tahitensis is popular at a very specific region of the world only.

Question 2

iris_gps <- read_csv("iris_gps.csv")

usa.map <- map_data(map = "usa")

ggplot(usa.map, aes(long,lat, group=group)) + geom_polygon(fill = "white", color = "gray90") + coord_quickmap(xlim = c(-66, -125), ylim = c(24, 50)) + geom_point(data= iris_gps, mapping = aes(x = lon, y = lat, group = species, color = species), size = 0.5)

Question 3

iris_gps_northeast_only <- iris_gps %>% filter(
  lon >= -72 & lon <= -66 & lat >= 43 & lat <= 45)

us_northeast_map <- usa.map %>%
  filter( long >= -72 & long <= -66 & lat >= 43 & lat <= 45)

ggplot(us_northeast_map, aes(long,lat, group=group)) + geom_polygon(fill = "white", color = "gray90") + coord_quickmap(xlim = c(-66, -72), ylim = c(43, 45)) + geom_point(data= iris_gps_northeast_only, mapping = aes(x = lon, y = lat, group = species, color = species), size = 0.5)

Question 4

It looks like Iris versicolor is widely in the northeast, while Iris virginica is focused in the central and southeast. Iris setosa is rarely seen in the US. It is possible that the species got separated by geographies, causing their individual speciation.

Question 5

ggplot(data = iris_gps, aes(x = month, y = after_stat(density), 
  group = species, fill = species)) +
  geom_histogram(position = "dodge",
  binwidth = 1) + 
  facet_wrap(~species, nrow = 3) + 
  xlim(1, 12) + theme_minimal() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq_along(month.name), 
    labels = month.name) +
  labs(x = "month", y = "density", title = "flowering time of iris species")
## Warning: Removed 1301 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_bin()`).

Evaluating reproductive isolation with this method can be problematic because flowers do not bloom for a whole month necessarily. Flowering time is usually a good marker for fertility of plants and reproductive transition, but these species might not overlap as much as this graph leads us to believe. Furthermore, most plants tend to bloom in the spring and summer, so it is not very surprising that different iris species do as well. # Part III: Chromosomal data

iris_chrom_dataset <- read_csv("iris.chromosome.csv", skip = 1, col_names = TRUE, trim_ws = TRUE, skip_empty_rows = TRUE)

head(iris_chrom_dataset)
## # A tibble: 6 × 15
##   id      source internal_id major_group family genus original_name matched_name
##   <chr>   <chr>        <dbl> <chr>       <chr>  <chr> <chr>         <chr>       
## 1 ipcn-a… ipcn-…    16600207 Angiosperms Irida… Iris  Iris acutilo… Iris acutil…
## 2 book-a… book-…          NA Angiosperms Irida… Iris  Iris acutilo… Iris acutil…
## 3 ipcn-a… ipcn-…    16600226 Angiosperms Irida… Iris  Iris ewbanki… Iris ewbank…
## 4 ipcn-a… ipcn-…    16600242 Angiosperms Irida… Iris  Iris lineola… Iris lineol…
## 5 ipcn-a… ipcn-…    16600242 Angiosperms Irida… Iris  Iris lineola… Iris lineol…
## 6 ipcn-a… ipcn-…    16602561 Angiosperms Irida… Iris  Iris acutilo… Iris acutil…
## # ℹ 7 more variables: resolved_name <chr>, taxonomic_status <chr>,
## #   gametophytic <chr>, sporophytic <chr>, parsed_n <chr>, reference <chr>,
## #   voucher <lgl>
iris_chrom <- iris_chrom_dataset %>%
    dplyr::select(resolved_name, sporophytic, parsed_n) %>%
    separate(resolved_name, into = c("genus", "species"), sep = " ", remove = TRUE, extra = "drop")

iris_chrom <- drop_na(iris_chrom)
#--------
setosa_chrom <- iris_chrom %>%
    filter(species == "setosa")

setosa_chrom
## # A tibble: 17 × 4
##    genus species sporophytic parsed_n  
##    <chr> <chr>   <chr>       <chr>     
##  1 Iris  setosa  "38"        19        
##  2 Iris  setosa  "38"        19        
##  3 Iris  setosa  "38"        19        
##  4 Iris  setosa  "38"        19        
##  5 Iris  setosa  "38"        19        
##  6 Iris  setosa  "38"        19        
##  7 Iris  setosa  "36"        18        
##  8 Iris  setosa  "38"        19        
##  9 Iris  setosa  "38"        19        
## 10 Iris  setosa  "38"        19        
## 11 Iris  setosa  "38"        19        
## 12 Iris  setosa  "34,36,38"  17, 18, 19
## 13 Iris  setosa  "38"        19        
## 14 Iris  setosa  "\"34-36\"" 17,18     
## 15 Iris  setosa  "38"        19        
## 16 Iris  setosa  "c. 54"     27        
## 17 Iris  setosa  "c. 54"     27
virginica_chrom <- iris_chrom %>%
    filter(species == "virginica")

virginica_chrom
## # A tibble: 2 × 4
##   genus species   sporophytic parsed_n  
##   <chr> <chr>     <chr>       <chr>     
## 1 Iris  virginica 70,72       35, 36    
## 2 Iris  virginica 54, 56, 70  27, 28, 35
versicolor_chrom <- iris_chrom %>%
    filter(species == "versicolor")

versicolor_chrom
## # A tibble: 5 × 4
##   genus species    sporophytic parsed_n
##   <chr> <chr>      <chr>       <chr>   
## 1 Iris  versicolor 108         54      
## 2 Iris  versicolor 108         54      
## 3 Iris  versicolor 108         54      
## 4 Iris  versicolor 108         54      
## 5 Iris  versicolor 72, 84, 105 36, 42

Question 1

iris_chrom <- iris_chrom %>% 
  mutate(sporophytic_clean = str_replace_all(iris_chrom$sporophytic, "2n =", ""))

iris_chrom <- iris_chrom %>% 
  dplyr::select(-sporophytic)

iris_chrom <- iris_chrom %>% mutate(good = str_detect(sporophytic_clean, "^...$|^..S"))

iris_good <- iris_chrom %>% filter(good == TRUE)
iris_bad <- iris_chrom %>% filter(good == FALSE)

iris_commas <- iris_bad %>% filter(str_detect(sporophytic_clean, ",") == TRUE)
iris_other <- iris_bad %>%
  filter(str_detect(sporophytic_clean, ",") == FALSE)

iris_commas_clean <- iris_commas %>% 
  slice(-10,-11,-12,-32,-44,-46,-49,-55,-58,-63) %>% 
  separate_longer_delim(c(sporophytic_clean, parsed_n), delim = ",")

clean_iris_chrom <- bind_rows(iris_good, iris_commas_clean) %>%
  dplyr::select(-good)

clean_iris_chrom$sporophytic_clean <- as.numeric(clean_iris_chrom$sporophytic_clean)

mode <- function(x){
    ux <- unique(x)
    ux[which.max(tabulate(match(x, ux)))]}

iris_chrom_mode <- clean_iris_chrom %>% group_by(species) %>% summarize(across(sporophytic_clean, mode))
iris_chrom_mode
## # A tibble: 41 × 2
##    species     sporophytic_clean
##    <chr>                   <dbl>
##  1 aphylla                    24
##  2 arenaria                   22
##  3 brevicaulis                42
##  4 confusa                    28
##  5 cristata                   24
##  6 danfordiae                 18
##  7 domestica                  28
##  8 ensata                     40
##  9 florentina                 22
## 10 graminea                   34
## # ℹ 31 more rows

Question 2

ggplot(data = iris_chrom_mode, aes(x = sporophytic_clean, y=after_stat(density), group = species)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "skyblue") + theme_minimal() + labs(x = "sporophytic chromosomes", title = "number of sporophytic chromosomes in iris species")

Question 3

Setosa has a much smaller amount of sporophytic chromosomes when compared to versicolor and virginica. The mode for setosa is 34, the mode for versicolor is 108, and the mode for virginica is 70. In fact, vesicolor’s chromosome amounts equals that of setosa and virginica together. It is possibe that versicolor is a hybrid of setosa and virginica.

Part IV: Producing a scientific argument

Question 1

Using morphological data, it seems that Iris setosa, Iris virginica, and Iris versicolor are significantly different when looking at petal width and sepal width as well as sepal length and petal length (based on approximate error rate seen in LDA). These two morphological traits were the best ones to differentiate between the three species, though it is clear that setosa is “more different” than the other two. Iris versicolor and Iris virginica tended to cluster together much more, meaning they have similar morphological traits, in graphs looking at morphological traits. Using geographic data, Iris setosa is clearly much more geographically isolated from the other two, shedding light on the potential reason as to why it is much more morphologically different. However, interestingly, versicolor and virginica, while overlapping, did seem to be mostly isolated to different geographic areas, especially when looking at the US. This illustrates that geographical distance between the three species is the likely driver of speciation thus far. Temporal data was presented as flowering time in months for each iris species. This data showed that all three flower around the same time (June), which makes sense since most flowering plants tend to flower at that time. Flowering time, while a good indicator of reproductive transition and fertility, is not as helpful when modeled per month. Flowering time can still vary within the month, and we may not see trends when we look at the flowering time data this generally. Perhaps if we were able to visuallize the data per day of the year for each species we would (or would not) see a larger different, but it would be more meaningful and accurate to represent the data in such a way. Chromosomal data shows that all iris species generally have around 20 (sporophytic) chromosomes. Interestingly, setosa had 34 chromosomes, virginica had 70, and versicolor had 108. The chromosome numbers of setosa and virginica approximately add up to the number of chromosomes of versicolor, supporting the theory that versicolor emerged as a hybrid between the two species.

The most compelling data to me is the geographical and chromosomal data. The temporal data, in my opinion, was not represented in a meaningful way. The morphological data was helpful, but speciation does not only depend on a phenotypic change, so it was only one part of the puzzle.

The original species designations made by Edgar Anderson were likely mostly correct, especially the distinction between setosa and the two others. However, it is entirely possible that he mistook versicolor for virginica and vise versa because of their proximity and small overlap in the Northeastern US and similar phenotypes.

The additional geographical and chromosomal data seemed to further prove Edgar’s assumptions correct, as each species has a different number of sporophytic chromosomes and they are all (mostly) geographically isolated from one another.

Therefore, Due to the gepgraphical separation of the species and the different chromosome numbers, I believe these organisms represent three distinct species, that might have just undergone a speciation event relatively not long ago.
